config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but it should not automatically come before individual rights, because rights are what protect people from abuse by majorities, governments, or powerful groups. In my view, a fair society is built by balancing both, not by sacrificing the individual whenever it is convenient. From my perspective, once you give society priority over rights too easily, it becomes too easy to justify injustice in the name of the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over individual rights, because in the United States we have seen how easily majorities can trample minorities when “the common good” is used as a blanket excuse. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every case, since laws for public safety, public health, and preventing harm do place real limits on personal freedom. The key point is that society should justify those limits carefully, not assume it has a default right to override the person. If we reverse that order, we risk turning rights into privileges that can be taken away whenever they become inconvenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a default rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how easily “for the common good” can be used to silence criticism, pressure minorities, or excuse abuse by authorities, so I do not trust that phrase when it is treated as absolute. At the same time, I do believe individual rights can be limited in narrow cases like public safety or preventing direct harm, but those limits must be justified clearly and kept narrow. If society always comes first, then rights stop being real protections and become permissions granted by those in power.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a U.S. perspective, I am wary of any argument that gives “the common good” automatic moral priority, because American history shows how easily that language can be used to excuse discrimination, censorship, or government overreach against unpopular groups. At the same time, I do not treat individual rights as unlimited, since things like public health rules, criminal law, and emergency measures can justify narrow restrictions when they are clearly necessary and proportionate. But the burden should always be on society to prove that a limit is justified, not on the individual to prove that their rights deserve to exist.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I am very cautious about any slogan about the common good, because in practice it can be used to pressure dissenters, silence minorities, or excuse abuse by officials and local power holders. I do think there are situations where society can limit individual freedom, such as public safety or preventing direct harm, but those limits must be narrow, transparent, and accountable. If we start from the idea that society comes first, it becomes too easy to treat individual rights as expendable instead of as the basic protection people need.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. In the United States, I do not trust any standard that gives “the common good” automatic supremacy, because history shows that majorities and governments can use that language to justify segregation, censorship, and other abuses against unpopular people. At the same time, I do not believe rights are limitless; public safety, public health, and preventing direct harm can justify narrow restrictions, but only with a strong burden of proof. If society gets priority by default, then rights stop being protections and become permissions that can be revoked whenever they are inconvenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
